description: The provided image is unreadable and appears to be a 'Check for updates' UI icon rather than the actual scientific figure. Based on the text extraction summary for the Fe-30Mn-10Al-1.5C-xBe (x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5) low-density steel study, this placeholder likely corresponds to the engineering stress-strain curves (Fig. 10). The caption states these curves illustrate tensile behavior for hot-rolled samples with varying Be additions. The extracted data reveal distinct mechanical responses: the 0Be baseline shows yield strength of 989.51 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 1222.27 MPa, and elongation of 22.91%; the 0.5Be alloy achieves optimal properties with yield strength of 1011.54 MPa, UTS of 1130.18 MPa, and maximum elongation of 40.47%; the 1.0Be steel exhibits reduced yield strength of 820.57 MPa and elongation of 30.71%; while the 1.5Be steel shows yield strength of 941.87 MPa but significantly decreased elongation of 18.33%. The trend demonstrates that 0.5 wt% Be addition provides the best strength-ductility balance with a product of approximately 45.5 GPa%, whereas higher Be content promotes brittleness due to excessive Be-Fe intermetallic precipitation.

This figure presents X-ray diffraction patterns of as-cast Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe (x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 wt%) low-density steels, illustrating the phase evolution with increasing beryllium content. The 0Be sample exhibits a single-phase austenitic (γ) structure with characteristic peaks indexed as (111), (200), (220), and (311). Upon adding 0.5% Be, the pattern remains predominantly austenitic with possible minor precipitate indications. At 1.0% Be, distinct diffraction peaks emerge corresponding to Be₁₁Fe and Be₂Fe intermetallic compounds, alongside the appearance of ferrite (δ) phase peaks. The 1.5Be sample displays the most complex phase constitution, showing clear signatures of austenite (γ), ferrite (δ), and multiple beryllide phases including Be₁₁Fe, Be₁₂Fe, Be₂Fe, and BeC. The systematic transition from single-phase austenite to a dual-phase matrix with increasing volume fraction of hard intermetallic precipitates confirms that Be addition promotes ferrite stabilization and formation of Be-Fe compounds, consistent with the microstructural evolution described in the associated text.

This figure presents optical micrographs (OM) showing the as-cast microstructural evolution of Fe-30Mn-10Al-1.5C-xBe low-density steels with varying beryllium additions: (a) 0Be, (b) 0.5Be, (c) 1.0Be, and (d) 1.5Be. Subfigure (a) displays a fully austenitic (γ) matrix with characteristic cast dendritic morphology. In (b), the 0.5Be alloy exhibits black point-like and needle-shaped beryllium compounds (Be-Fe phase) precipitating within the austenite matrix. Subfigures (c) and (d) reveal a dual-phase microstructure consisting of austenite (γ) and ferrite (δ), accompanied by distinctive Chinese character-shaped Be-Fe intermetallic compounds (identified as Be12Fe) predominantly located at austenite-ferrite boundaries. As Be content increases from 1.0 wt% to 1.5 wt%, the volume fraction of Be-Fe compounds increases significantly, encroaching upon the austenite matrix and leading to reduced ferrite content and refined ferrite grain size in the 1.5Be sample relative to 1.0Be. All micrographs share a 50 μm scale bar.

Figure 3 presents bright-field TEM micrographs and selected area electron diffraction patterns (SAED) identifying Be12Fe precipitates in as-cast Fe-30Mn-10Al-1.5C-0.5Be low-density steel. Panel (a) shows the overall morphology at low magnification (scale bar 5 μm), revealing distinct precipitate structures distributed in the matrix, with red and green boxes marking regions A and B for detailed examination. Panel (b) provides an enlarged view of area A (scale bar 1 μm), displaying blocky or irregular precipitate morphology with the SAED acquisition site indicated by a circle labeled SADP. Panel (c) shows the enlarged view of area B (scale bar 1 μm), featuring elongated needle-like or rod-like precipitates with the corresponding SAED location similarly marked. Panels (d) and (e) present the SAED patterns acquired from the precipitates in areas A and B, respectively. Both patterns confirm the intermetallic phase as Be12Fe, with (d) indexed along the [−113] zone axis showing {301}, {152}, and {2̄51} reflections, and (e) indexed along the [001] zone axis exhibiting {101} and {202} reflections. This figure provides direct crystallographic evidence for the formation of Be12Fe compounds in the 0.5Be as-cast steel, supporting the conclusion that beryllium addition promotes Be-Fe intermetallic precipitation at the boundaries between austenite and ferrite.

This figure presents bright-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and a selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of as-cast Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–1.5Be low-density steel. Subfigures (a) and (b) display different morphologies of Be₁₂Fe precipitates, exhibiting large, intersecting needle-like and plate-like structures that form characteristic Chinese-character patterns distributed within the austenite matrix. Subfigure (c) shows the morphology of AlFe₃ intermetallic compound, with a circle marking the selected area for diffraction analysis. Subfigure (d) presents the corresponding SAED pattern indexed as AlFe₃ along the [011] zone axis, with labeled diffraction spots including {111}, {1̄11}, and {200} planes, confirming the cubic crystal structure of this phase. All micrographs include 1 μm scale bars. The images collectively demonstrate that high Be addition (1.5 wt%) in the as-cast steel leads to formation of coarse Be₁₂Fe precipitates and AlFe₃ intermetallics, which occupy matrix positions and contribute to the complex microstructural evolution in Be-bearing low-density steels.

This bar chart presents the Vickers microhardness (HV) of as-cast Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe (x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5) low-density steels, comparing hardness values between austenite (red bars) and ferrite (blue bars) phases across different beryllium contents. The x-axis denotes Be addition levels (0Be, 0.5Be, 1.0Be, 1.5Be), while the y-axis shows hardness in HV. For the fully austenitic 0Be and 0.5Be steels, measured austenite hardness values are 453.31 HV and 462.88 HV respectively, with no ferrite present. In the dual-phase 1.0Be and 1.5Be steels, austenite hardness increases to 475.80 HV and 479.74 HV, while ferrite hardness registers 437.40 HV and 452.54 HV respectively. The data reveals a monotonic increase in austenite hardness with increasing Be content, alongside the emergence of softer ferrite phases at higher Be levels (≥1.0 wt%). This supports the conclusion that Be addition strengthens the matrix through solid solution and compound formation, though the hardness increment remains modest despite significant compound precipitation, while the appearance of ferrite in 1.0Be and 1.5Be steels creates a dual-phase microstructure with distinct hardness partitioning between phases.

This figure displays DSC (Differential Scanning Calorimetry) heating curves for Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels with varying beryllium contents (x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, and 1.5 wt%), measured after homogenisation. The graph presents four distinct endothermic peaks indicating phase transformation temperatures during heating. The base 0Be alloy shows a peak at 1320 °C, while the caption reports a transformation temperature of 1278.8 °C for this composition. Upon adding beryllium, the transformation temperature drops sharply: the 0.5Be alloy peaks at 1165.9 °C, the 1.0Be alloy at 1163.3 °C, and the 1.5Be alloy at 1154.8 °C. The data demonstrates that beryllium addition reduces the phase transformation temperature by approximately 120 °C, with the most significant decrease occurring at 0.5 wt% Be, followed by minor reductions of roughly 1–5 °C as the Be content increases further. This confirms that Be substantially alters the thermal stability and lowers the processing temperatures for these austenitic low-density steels.

X-ray diffraction patterns of Fe-30Mn-10Al-1.5C-xBe (x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 wt%) low-density steel strips after hot rolling at 1050 °C, illustrating the phase evolution with increasing beryllium content. The 0Be sample shows characteristic austenite (γ) peaks indexed as (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222). At 0.5Be, the pattern remains predominantly austenitic with no observable ferrite peaks. Upon increasing Be to 1.0 wt%, distinct δ-ferrite (200) reflections appear alongside Be-Fe intermetallic compound peaks including Be11Fe (112, 212) and Be2Fe. The 1.5Be sample exhibits the most complex phase assemblage with simultaneous presence of austenite, ferrite, Be11Fe, and Be2Fe phases, indicating that excess Be precipitates as intermetallic compounds when the austenite matrix reaches saturation. The systematic emergence of ferrite and Be-rich phases with increasing Be content confirms the transition from single-phase to dual-phase microstructure, supporting the proposed solid solubility limits and precipitation sequence in Be-bearing low-density steels.

This figure presents optical microscopy (OM) images showing the microstructural evolution of Fe-30Mn-10Al-1.5C-xBe low-density steels (with x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, and 1.5 wt% Be) after hot rolling at 1050°C. The four subfigures (a)-(d) correspond to increasing beryllium content: (a) 0Be exhibits a fully austenitic (γ-Fe) matrix with annealing twins and fine precipitates; (b) 0.5Be maintains the austenitic structure but introduces dispersed Be-Fe phase particles; (c) 1.0Be reveals a dual-phase microstructure comprising austenite and elongated ferrite (α-Fe) bands along the rolling direction (RD), with visible Be-Fe phases at grain boundaries; (d) 1.5Be shows a more pronounced dual-phase structure with increased volume fraction of Be-Fe intermetallic compounds. All images include a 50 μm scale bar and RD indicator. The progression demonstrates that Be addition promotes ferrite formation and modifies precipitate distribution, transforming the steel from single-phase austenite to austenite-ferrite duplex microstructure while refining grain morphology along the rolling direction.

Fig. 9 presents combined SEM morphology and EDS elemental mapping of Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–1.5Be low-density steel after hot rolling at 1050 °C. Subfigure (a) shows the SEM image revealing the junction between austenite and ferrite phases, with a rectangular region A highlighting an elongated strip-shaped structure. Subfigures (b–e) display the corresponding EDS elemental distribution maps for Fe (magenta), Mn (cyan), C (dark), and Al (yellow-brown), respectively. The EDS maps demonstrate that the inner strip-shaped structure (region A) is enriched in both Mn and Al, while the outer region of the strip shows Mn enrichment but Al depletion. According to the caption and text, this elongated Mn-rich and Al-rich structure corresponds to the ferrite (δ-ferrite) phase in the 1.5Be steel, surrounded by austenitic matrix. The carbon map shows relatively weak signal intensity. All images share a 1 μm scale bar, indicating microstructural features at the micrometer scale. This figure provides critical evidence for identifying the dual-phase microstructure and elemental partitioning between ferrite and austenite in Be-bearing low-density steels.

Figure 10 presents the tensile mechanical properties of hot-rolled (1050 °C) Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe (x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5) low-density steels. Subfigure (a) displays engineering stress-strain curves for four Be contents: 0Be (black), 0.5Be (red), 1.0Be (blue), and 1.5Be (green). The 0.5Be alloy exhibits superior ductility with an elongation of approximately 40.47% and a yield strength of ~1012 MPa, while the 0Be baseline shows ~23% elongation with yield strength ~990 MPa. The 1.0Be steel displays reduced yield strength (~821 MPa) but maintains moderate elongation (~31%), whereas 1.5Be shows recovered strength (~942 MPa yield) but decreased ductility (~18%). The inset illustrates the dog-bone tensile specimen geometry with gauge length 50 mm, width 20 mm, and thickness 3 mm. Subfigure (b) plots strain hardening rate (dσ/dε) versus true strain, revealing that 0.5Be maintains the lowest steady-state hardening rate (~1.4–1.6 GPa), while higher Be contents exhibit elevated hardening rates (~2.0–2.2 GPa). These curves demonstrate that optimal Be addition (0.5 wt%) enhances the strength-ductility balance through solution strengthening and fine Be–Fe precipitation, whereas excessive Be promotes ferrite formation and brittle compounds, reducing uniform elongation.

This bar chart presents the matrix (austenite) hardness of Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe (x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5) low-density steels in the as-rolled condition, measured using an FM-ARS9000 Vickers hardness tester. The x-axis denotes beryllium content (0Be, 0.5Be, 1.0Be, 1.5Be), while the y-axis indicates hardness in HV. The 0.5Be alloy exhibits the highest austenite hardness of 470.97 HV, significantly exceeding the 415.55 HV of the Be-free steel. Notably, the hardness drops to 398.39 HV at 1.0Be, then slightly recovers to 407.01 HV at 1.5Be, indicating a non-monotonic response to Be addition. This trend correlates with microstructural evolution revealed in accompanying data: the 0.5Be steel contains dispersed Be11Fe precipitates and exhibits grain coarsening (>20 µm), while higher Be contents promote ferrite formation and different Be-Fe compound distributions (Be11Fe, Be12Fe, Be2Fe, Be2C). The hardness values, presented with error bars representing measurement variability, support the conclusion that optimal Be addition (0.5 wt%) enhances matrix strength through precipitation hardening, whereas excessive Be leads to hardness reduction due to ferrite softening.

This figure presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of tensile fracture surfaces for hot-rolled Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels with varying beryllium contents: (a) 0Be, (b) 0.5Be, (c) 1.0Be, and (d) 1.5Be. Subfigure (a) at 25 μm scale shows ductile fracture characterized by dimpled morphology; the inset highlights Area A1 (enlarged view showing spherical inclusions within dimples) and Area A2 (exhibiting dissociation planes and river patterns). Subfigure (b) at 50 μm scale displays similar ductile features for 0.5Be steel. Subfigures (c) and (d), both at 50 μm scale, reveal a transition toward brittle fracture mechanisms as Be content increases, with subfigure (c) showing emerging cleavage platforms and subfigure (d) explicitly marking prominent cleavage steps indicative of quasi-cleavage fracture. The progression from ductile dimple rupture in 0Be and 0.5Be steels to mixed-mode fracture with extensive cleavage planes in 1.0Be and 1.5Be steels demonstrates that Be addition promotes brittle fracture characteristics, correlating with reduced elongation observed in mechanical testing.

This figure presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) characterization of Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe (x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5) low-density steels hot-rolled at 1050 °C, arranged in a 4×4 matrix. Rows (a)–(d) correspond to increasing Be content (0Be, 0.5Be, 1.0Be, and 1.5Be), while columns display distinct EBSD mappings: Column 1 shows Image Quality (IQ) maps with grain boundaries color-coded by misorientation angles (blue >15°, green 5°–15°, red 1°–5°); Column 2 presents Inverse Pole Figure (IPF) orientation maps revealing crystallographic textures relative to the rolling direction (RD); Column 3 displays Kernel Average Misorientation (KAM) maps indicating local stress concentrations, particularly at carbide and Be–Fe phase precipitates located at grain boundaries and phase interfaces; and Column 4 shows phase maps where green denotes austenite and red indicates ferrite. The microstructural evolution demonstrates a transition from single-phase austenite (0Be) to dual-phase austenite-ferrite structures (1.0Be and 1.5Be) with increasing Be content, accompanied by grain refinement and altered precipitation distributions. The KAM maps reveal that stress concentration becomes more uniformly distributed in higher-Be alloys (1.0Be, 1.5Be), correlating with the formation of ferrite and Be-containing intermetallic phases.

Figure 14 presents grain size distribution histograms obtained by EBSD analysis for hot-rolled Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels with varying beryllium content: (a) 0Be, (b) 0.5Be, (c) 1.0Be, and (d) 1.5Be. Subfigure (a) shows a unimodal distribution peaking at approximately 15 μm with about 55% area fraction concentrated in the 12–18 μm range. Subfigure (b) exhibits significant grain coarsening with a broad distribution shifted toward larger diameters, peaking near 40 μm, where over 75% of the area fraction exceeds 20 μm. Subfigure (c) demonstrates marked grain refinement with a sharp peak at approximately 10 μm and an average grain size of ~11 μm. Subfigure (d) displays a bimodal-like distribution with a primary peak at ~12–13 μm (average ~13 μm) and a secondary population around 25 μm, indicating heterogeneous grain growth. These statistics reveal that 0.5 wt% Be addition causes abnormal grain coarsening, while 1.0 wt% Be effectively refines the microstructure, and 1.5 wt% Be results in non-uniform grain size distribution, which correlates with the observed mechanical property variations across the alloy series.

Figure 15 presents bright-field TEM micrographs and corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns for Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe (x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5) low-density steels after hot rolling. Subfigures (a1)–(d1) display the general microstructures, revealing recrystallized austenite grains with annealing twins and varying precipitate distributions across compositions. Subfigures (a2), (b2), and (d2) provide SAED patterns indexed to the face-centered cubic austenite matrix (γ-Fe) along the [114], [111], and [013] zone axes, respectively. Subfigures (a3)–(d3) offer higher-magnification views of carbide morphologies, while (a4)–(d4) present SAED patterns identifying specific carbide phases. The 0Be steel contains κ-carbide (a4, [001] zone axis), whereas Be-containing steels exhibit Be2C precipitates (b4: [112], c4: [011], d4: [112]). Subfigure (c2) additionally shows precipitate distribution in 1.0Be steel. These results demonstrate that Be addition fundamentally alters the carbide phase constitution from κ-carbide to Be2C.

Bright-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images displaying dislocation structures in hot-rolled Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels with varying beryllium content. Subfigure (a) shows the 0.5Be steel exhibiting sparse dislocation lines (indicated by white arrows) within the austenitic matrix. Subfigure (b) presents the 1.0Be steel revealing increased dislocation density and tangles (arrows) within the dual-phase austenite-ferrite microstructure. Subfigure (c) illustrates the 1.5Be steel with dense dislocation networks and complex dislocation configurations (arrows) concentrated at grain boundaries and phase interfaces. All micrographs share a 2 μm scale bar. The progressive increase in dislocation density from 0.5Be to 1.5Be visually confirms that Be addition enhances dislocation density, contributing to dislocation strengthening of the steel, consistent with cited EBSD Kernel Average Misorientation (KAM) statistics. The samples were processed via hot rolling at 1050 °C with 83% total reduction followed by water quenching.

This schematic diagram illustrates the evolution of grain structure and precipitate distribution in hot-rolled Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels as a function of increasing beryllium content from 0 to 1.5 wt%. Panel (a) depicts the 0Be alloy consisting of single-phase austenite (γ) grains containing annealing twins. Panel (b) shows the 0.5Be alloy where small precipitated phases (orange dots identified as Be11Fe and Be2C in the text) begin to form within the austenite matrix. Panel (c) represents the 1.0Be alloy exhibiting a dual-phase microstructure with the appearance of ferrite (α, green regions) alongside austenite, accompanied by increased precipitation at grain boundaries. Panel (d) displays the 1.5Be alloy with a higher volume fraction of ferrite and more abundant precipitated phases (including Be11Fe, Be12Fe, Be2Fe, and Be2C) distributed both at grain boundaries and within grains. The sequence demonstrates that Be addition progressively transforms the microstructure from single-phase austenite to a dual-phase steel with increasing amounts of intermetallic precipitates and ferrite formation, explaining the observed changes in strength, ductility, and density.

This figure presents three schematic crystal structure models illustrating the solid solution behavior of Be, Al, and C in Fe-Mn-Al-C-Be low-density steels. The first model (left) depicts a substitutional solid solution where the smaller Be atom (blue) substitutes for Fe (green), causing surrounding Fe atoms to shift inward and resulting in lattice contraction. The second model (middle) shows Al (orange) substitution, where the larger Al atom radius causes outward displacement of neighboring Fe atoms, leading to lattice expansion distinct from the Be-induced distortion. The third model (right) illustrates interstitial solid solution with C atoms (purple) occupying interstitial sites in the Fe lattice. These diagrams visualize how Be addition creates lattice shrinkage rather than expansion, explaining why Be (despite its low density of 1.87 g/cm³) cannot effectively reduce steel density like Al does. The models also rationalize the observed carbon precipitation behavior, where Be-induced lattice distortion excludes C atoms from interstitial positions, promoting carbide formation and limiting solid solubility, ultimately affecting the microstructure stability and solubility limits in the austenite matrix.

This figure presents the binary Fe–Be phase diagram calculated under thermodynamic equilibrium conditions, illustrating the relationship between composition (molar ratio of Fe/(Be+Fe)) and temperature. The horizontal axis spans from pure Be (0) to pure Fe (1), while the vertical axis represents temperature from 0 to 1800 °C. The diagram identifies multiple phase regions including Liquid, body-centered cubic BCC-A2 (δ-ferrite), hexagonal close-packed HCP-A3 (Be-rich terminal solid solution), and face-centered cubic FCC-A1 (γ-austenite). Notably, three intermetallic compounds are marked: FeBe₁₂ (Be-rich), FeBe₅, and FeBe₂ (Fe-rich). The diagram reveals extremely limited solid solubility of Be in austenite (<1 mol% Fe), with Be preferentially forming intermetallic compounds rather than dissolving in the FCC matrix. Conversely, the BCC-A2 ferrite phase exhibits significantly higher Be solubility. This thermodynamic data supports the experimental observation that excess Be in Fe–Mn–Al–C–Be steels precipitates as brittle Be–Fe compounds within the austenite matrix or at grain boundaries, while partial Be dissolution occurs in ferrite when present.

This scatter plot (Fig. 20) compares the product of tensile strength and elongation (strength-ductility product, GPa·%) versus density (g/cm³) for Be-bearing low-density steels against conventional and other lightweight steels. The x-axis ranges from 6.2 to 8.0 g/cm³ and the y-axis from 5 to 50 GPa·%. The Fe-30Mn-10Al-1.5C-xBe alloys (x = 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 wt%), marked by distinct symbols (square, circle, triangle, hexagon), occupy the upper-left region with densities of ~6.29–6.47 g/cm³ and strength-ductility products reaching ~46 GPa·% for the 0.5Be composition. These values significantly surpass conventional steels (40Cr, GCr15, 0Cr13, 1Cr23Ni18, Q235, etc.), which cluster at higher densities (7.6–7.9 g/cm³) with lower products (8–25 GPa·%). Other Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels (e.g., Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-3Cr, Fe-28Mn-9Al-1.2C-9Cr) plot at intermediate positions. A dashed curve suggests a performance envelope, with the 0.5Be steel approaching the optimum. The figure demonstrates that Be steels achieve a 21.38% density reduction compared to pure iron while maintaining exceptional mechanical properties, defining a new space in the density-strength-ductility relationship.
